sections.heading: Power of the Cabinet Secretary to issue rules and regulations ......................................................................................... 15
sections.title: Cabinet Secretary's power to issue capital markets rules
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary may issue rules and regulations to govern listing and de-listing of securities, disclosures by market participants, record-keeping requirements, and other capital market activities. These rules must align with promoting an efficient securities market and be publicly exposed for 30 days for stakeholder feedback. Stakeholders include listed companies and entities licensed by the Authority with operations affecting capital markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Rules require public consultation before implementation and define stakeholders for capital markets regulation, impacting how data transfers and processing are governed within Kenya's regulatory framework.

requirements.text: The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate rules and regulations to regulate listing and de-listing of securities on a securities exchange
The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate rules and regulations to regulate disclosures about securities transactions by stockbrokers, derivatives brokers and dealers
The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate rules and regulations to regulate the keeping and proper maintenance of books, records, accounts and audits by all persons approved or licensed by the Authority
All rules and regulations formulated under subsection (1) shall take into account and be consistent with the objective of promoting and maintaining an effective and efficient securities market

All rules and regulations formulated under subsection (1) shall be exposed for comment by stakeholders and the general public for a period of thirty days through notification in at least two daily newspapers of national circulation and the electronic media

Stakeholders shall include listed companies and all persons licensed or approved by the Authority or financial or other institutions whose operations have, in the opinion of the Authority, a bearing on the development and regulation of capital markets in Kenya

sections.source_quote: The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate such rules and regulations as may be required to regulate— (a) listing and de-listing of securities on a securities exchange; (b) disclosures about securities transactions by— (i) stockbrokers, derivatives brokers and dealers; (ii) persons who acquire or dispose of securities or exchange-traded derivatives contracts; (iii) a securities exchange;

sections.heading: Furnishing of information to the Authority .............................................................................................................................. 18
sections.title: Information disclosure to the Authority
sections.summary: The Authority may require persons to provide information in writing within a specified period. The Authority may not disclose information to third parties except for its objectives unless required by court. The Authority may share information with foreign regulatory bodies under specific conditions, including cost-sharing and reciprocal assistance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs data sharing with regulatory authorities, impacting cross-border data transfers and compliance with international regulatory requests.

requirements.text: The Authority or any person officially authorized in that behalf by the Authority may, by notice in writing, require any person to furnish to the Authority or to the authorized person, within such period as is specified in the notice, all such returns or information as specified in such notice

The Authority or any member thereof, or any officer or servant of the Authority, shall not disclose to any person or use any return or information acquired under subsection (1) except for the purpose of achieving the objectives of the Authority unless required to do so by a court of law

The Authority may, where it receives a request from a regulatory body, whether established within or outside Kenya, for assistance in investigating a person specified by the regulatory body who has contravened or is contravening any legal or regulatory requirements which— (a) are enforced or administers by that regulatory body; or (b) relate to securities transactions regulated by that regulatory body
The Authority may provide the assistance requested for by exercising any of its powers under this Act or by providing such other assistance as the Authority may consider necessary
A regulatory body which requests for assistance under subsection (3) shall demonstrate that it is desirable or expedient that the assistance requested should be provided in the interest of the public
A regulatory body which requests for assistance under subsection (3) shall demonstrate that the assistance shall assist the regulatory body in the discharge and performance of its functions

The Authority shall, in deciding whether the requirement under subsection (5) is satisfied in a particular case, take into account whether the regulatory body shall pay the Authority any of the costs and expenses incurred in providing the assistance

The Authority shall, in deciding whether the requirement under subsection (5) is satisfied in a particular case, take into account whether the regulatory body shall be able and willing to provide reciprocal assistance within its jurisdiction in response to a similar request for assistance from Kenya

sections.source_quote: The Authority or any person officially authorized in that behalf by the Authority may, by notice in writing, require any person to furnish to the Authority or to the authorized person, within such period as is specified in the notice, all such returns or information as specified in such notice.

sections.heading: Establishment of the Investor Compensation Fund ............................................................................................................... 21
sections.title: Investor Compensation Fund establishment for stockbroker failures
sections.summary: This section mandates the creation of an Investor Compensation Fund to compensate investors who suffer financial loss due to licensed stockbrokers or dealers failing to meet contractual obligations. The Fund is funded through contributions from licensed persons, fines, penalties, interest earnings, recovered sums, and approved external sources. The Authority may reward individuals who provide timely information leading to Fund recovery, with rewards capped at five million shillings. Offenders colluding with the Authority to collect rewards face fines up to five million shillings or imprisonment for five years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a fund for investor compensation but does not directly impact data transfers. It is irrelevant to Data Transfer Impact Assessments as it concerns financial compensation mechanisms rather than data handling.

requirements.text: There shall be established a Fund to be known as the Investor Compensation Fund for the purposes of granting compensation to investors who suffer pecuniary loss resulting from the failure of a licensed stockbroker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations.

The Compensation Fund shall consist of— (a) such moneys as are required to be paid into the Compensation Fund by licensed persons; (b) such sums of money as are paid under this Act as fines or penalties or under section 34 as ill-gotten gains where those harmed are not specifically identifiable; (c) such sums of money as accrue from interest and profits from investing Compensation Fund moneys; (d) such sums of money recovered by or on behalf of the Authority from entities whose failure to meet their obligations to investors result in payments from the Compensation Fund; (e) interest deemed to accrue on the proceeds of a public issue or offer for sale of shares of a company listed or to be listed on an approved securities exchange, between the closing date and the date of dispatch of refund cheques, or, where there is no refund, the date of dispatch of share certificates or crediting of securities accounts, to be determined at the rate prescribed by the Authority; (f) such sums of money as are received for purposes of the Compensation Fund from any other source approved by the Cabinet Secretary.

The Authority may reward any person who provides new and timely information leading to the recovery of sums of money referred to in subsection (2): Provided that— (a) this provision shall not apply to any officer of the Authority; (b) the reward payable under this subsection shall be three per cent of the amount recovered subject to a maximum of five million shillings; and (c) the reward referred to in paragraph (a) shall be paid before the recovered sums of money are transferred to the Fund.

A person who— (a) colludes with an officer of the Authority for the purpose of collecting the reward under subsection (2A); (b) while working at the Authority aids another person to get and provide information under subsection (2A); (c) provides false information under subsection (2A), commits an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years.

sections.source_quote: 18. Establishment of the Investor Compensation Fund (1) There shall be established a Fund to be known as the Investor Compensation Fund for the purposes of granting compensation to investors who suffer pecuniary loss resulting from the failure of a licensed stockbroker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations.

sections.heading: Disciplinary action by securities exchange ............................................................................................................................... 27
sections.title: Disciplinary action by securities exchanges
sections.summary: Securities exchanges must notify the Authority within seven days of taking disciplinary action against trading participants or listed companies. The Authority may review such actions, affirm or set aside decisions after providing opportunities for hearing. The Authority can also take disciplinary action itself if an exchange fails to act, but must give prior notice and opportunity to be heard.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires exchanges to report disciplinary actions to the regulator; regulator can override exchange decisions; regulator may directly discipline participants if exchanges fail to act.

requirements.text: Securities exchanges must notify the Authority within seven days of taking disciplinary action against trading participants or listed companies.
The Authority may review disciplinary actions taken by exchanges and affirm or set aside decisions after providing opportunities for hearing.
The Authority can take disciplinary action itself if an exchange fails to act, but must give prior notice and opportunity to be heard.

sections.source_quote: Where a securities exchange or a derivatives exchange reprimands, fines, suspends or expels, or otherwise takes disciplinary action against a trading participant or a listed company, it shall within seven days give notice to the Authority in writing, giving particulars including the name of the person, the reason for and nature of the action taken.

sections.heading: Procedure for collective investment schemes .......................................................................................................................... 38
sections.title: Collective investment schemes and securities offers
sections.summary: Collective investment schemes must be registered with the Authority before operating in Kenya. Promoters must apply for consent to register within three months of receiving consent, providing proof of legal constitution, incorporation documents, and registration applications. The Authority registers schemes that comply with requirements and issues certificates of registration. Schemes must publish information memoranda before offering shares publicly, and must comply with disclosure obligations to ensure transparency in securities transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the registration and operation of collective investment schemes, which impacts data transfer by requiring detailed registration and disclosure processes for entities handling financial data across borders.

requirements.text: No person may carry on business as a collective investment scheme in or from within Kenya without registration
Promoters must apply for consent to register a collective investment scheme upon complying with requirements
Promoters must deliver satisfactory proof of legal constitution within three months of consent
Promoters must submit proposed incorporation documents and other information as required by the Authority
Promoters must submit an application for registration in prescribed form with prescribed fee
The Authority registers collective investment schemes that comply with requirements and issues certificate of registration
A mutual fund deemed to be incorporated as a collective investment scheme with variable capital upon registration
Investment companies may operate without registration for six months from commencement of the Act
Investment companies must apply for registration before expiration of six-month period
Registered collective investment schemes may not offer shares publicly without publishing information memorandum
Information memoranda must comply with Authority requirements
Issuers must keep Authority, members, other holders, listing exchanges, and public informed of material information

sections.source_quote: No person shall carry on any business or engage in any activity as a collective investment scheme, in or from within Kenya, unless such person is registered under this Act. The promoters of a collective investment scheme that is proposed to be formed, may apply to the Authority for consent to register a collective investment scheme upon complying with the requirements prescribed under this Act.

sections.heading: Register of interest in securities ................................................................................................................................................... 53
sections.title: Registration of securities interests and insider trading prohibitions
sections.summary: This section mandates that licensed persons and financial journalists maintain a register of securities interests within seven days of acquisition or change. The Authority may inspect this register. It also defines insider trading, inside information, and market manipulation offenses, including penalties for contravening these rules. The section specifies that insider information must relate to securities, not be public, and likely to affect prices. It prohibits fraudulent activities, false statements, and market manipulation to prevent insider trading and ensure transparency in securities transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: The registration requirements and insider trading prohibitions impact cross-border data transfers involving securities by requiring transparency and authorization for transfers, which are critical for DTIA compliance in financial data contexts.

requirements.text: A person to whom section (1) applies shall maintain a register of the securities in which he or she has an interest and such interest or any changes in such interest shall be entered in the register within seven days of the acquisition or change in the interest.

The Authority or any person authorized by it in that behalf may require any person to whom section (1) applies to produce for inspection the register required under subsection (4) and the Authority or any person so authorized may make extracts from the register.

A person who deals in listed securities or their derivatives that are price-affected in relation to the information in his possession commits an offence of insider trading if that person encourages another person, whether or not that other person knows it, to deal in securities or their derivatives which are price-affected securities in relation to the information in the possession of the insider, knowing or having reasonable cause to believe that the trading would take place; or discloses the information, otherwise than in the proper performance of the functions of his employment, office or profession, to another person.

For the purposes of this Part, 'inside information' means information which relates to particular securities or to a particular issuer of securities; has not been made public; and if it were made public is likely to have a material effect on the price of the securities.

A person who enters into or carries out, directly or indirectly, two or more transactions in the securities of a company, or in other listed securities, which by themselves or in conjunction with any other transaction increase, reduce, or stabilize the price with the intention of inducing another person to purchase, sell, or refrain from purchasing or selling securities, commits an offence.

sections.source_quote: 32. Register of interest in securities (4) A person to whom section (1) applies shall maintain a register of the securities in which he or she has an interest and such interest or any changes in such interest shall be entered in the register within seven days of the acquisition or change in the interest.

sections.heading: Exemption from Cap. 446 ................................................................................................................................................................ 68
sections.title: Exemption from Cap. 446
sections.summary: This section provides an exemption from Cap. 446, which likely refers to another statute or regulation. The specific exemption details are not elaborated in the text, as the section appears to be a heading with no substantive content beyond the exemption statement.

requirements.text: Exemption from Cap. 446

sections.source_quote: Exemption from Cap. 446

sections.heading: Interpretation (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of key financial terms in capital markets
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Capital Markets Act, including 'substantial shareholder', 'trading participant', 'trustee', 'underwriting', and 'unit trust'. These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding securities transactions, ownership structures, and trust arrangements within Kenya's capital markets. The definitions apply to persons involved in securities trading, investment schemes, and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: information is regularly provided concerning the prices at which, or the consideration for which, particular persons, or particular classes of persons, propose, or may reasonably be expected, to sell, purchase or exchange securities
"substantial shareholder" means any person who is the beneficial owner of, or is in a position to exert control over, not less than fifteen per cent of the shares of a body corporate
"trading participant" means a licensed person with rights to trade at an approved securities exchange

"trustee" in relation to real estate investment trust or a collective investment scheme, means a person appointed under a trust deed of a real estate investment trust or a collective investment scheme, as the case may be, as its trustee
"underwriting" means the purchase or commitment to purchase or distribute by dealers or other persons of issue or offer of securities for immediate or prompt public distribution by or through them

"unit trust" means any scheme or arrangement in the nature of a trust in pursuance whereof members of the public are invited or permitted, as beneficiaries under the trust, to acquire an interest or undivided share (unit of investment) in one or more groups or blocks of specified securities and to participate proportionately in the income or profits derived therefrom

sections.source_quote: Capital Markets Act (Cap. 485A) Kenya (c) information is regularly provided concerning the prices at which, or the consideration for which, particular persons, or particular classes of persons, propose, or may reasonably be expected, to sell, purchase or exchange securities; "substantial shareholder" means any person who is the beneficial owner of, or is in a position to exert control over, not less than fifteen per cent of the shares of a body corporate; "trading participant" means a licensed person with rights to trade at an approved securities exchange; "trustee" in relation to real estate investment trust or a collective investment scheme, means a person appointed under a trust deed of a real estate investment trust or a collective investment scheme, as the case may be, as its trustee; "underwriting" means the purchase or commitment to purchase or distribute by dealers or other persons of issue or offer of securities for immediate or prompt public distribution by or through them; "unit trust" means any scheme or arrangement in the nature of a trust in pursuance whereof members of the public are invited or permitted, as beneficiaries under the trust, to acquire an interest or undivided share (unit of investment) in one or more groups or blocks of specified securities and to participate proportionately in the income or profits derived therefrom.

sections.heading: Definition of "interest in securities"
sections.title: Conditions for deeming a person to have an interest in securities
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions under which a person is deemed to have an interest in securities, including situations where they have contractual rights to purchase, transfer, or control securities, or where they have influence over a body corporate that holds securities. The rules apply to individuals and entities involved in securities transactions and trust arrangements, particularly in Kenya's capital markets framework.

requirements.text: Where any property held in trust consists of or includes securities in which a person knows, or has reasonable grounds for believing, that he or she has an interest, he or she shall be deemed to have an interest in those securities

A person shall be deemed to have an interest in a security where a body corporate has an interest in a security and— (a) the body corporate is, or its directors are, accustomed or under an obligation, whether formal or informal, to act in accordance with directions, instructions or wishes of that person in relation to that security; (b) that person has a controlling interest in the body corporate; or (c) that person is, or the associates of that person or that person and his associates are, entitled to exercise or control the exercise of not less than fifteen per cent of the votes attached to the voting shares in the body corporate

A person shall be deemed to have an interest in a security in any one or more of the following circumstances— (a) where he or she has entered into a contract to purchase a security; (b) where he or she has a right, otherwise than by reason of having an interest under a trust, to have a security transferred to himself or to his order, whether the right is exercisable presently or in the future and whether on the fulfillment of a condition or not; (c) where he or she has the right to acquire a security, or an interest in a security, under an option, whether on the fulfillment of a condition or not; or (d) where he or she is entitled, otherwise than by reason of his having been appointed a proxy or representative to vote at a meeting of members of a body corporate or of a class of its members, to exercise or control the exercise of a right attached to a security, not being a security of which he or she is the registered holder
A person shall be deemed to have an interest in a security if that security is held jointly with another person
For the purpose of determining whether a person has an interest in a security, it is immaterial that the interest cannot be related to a particular security

There shall be disregarded— (a) an interest in a security if the interest is that of a person who holds the security as bare trustee; (b) an interest in a security of a person whose ordinary business includes the lending of money if he or she holds the interest only by way of security for the purposes of a transaction entered into in the ordinary course of business in connection with the lending of money; (c) an interest of a person in a security being an interest held by him or her by reason of his holding a prescribed office; and (d) a prescribed interest in a security being an interest of such person, or of the persons included in such class of persons as is prescribed

An interest in a security shall not be disregarded by reason only of— (a) its remoteness; (b) the manner in which it arose; or (c) the fact that the exercise of a right conferred by the interest is, or is capable of being made, subject to restraint or restriction

sections.source_quote: Capital Markets Act (Cap. 485A) Kenya 4. Definition of "interest in securities" (1) Where any property held in trust consists of or includes securities in which a person knows, or has reasonable grounds for believing, that he or she has an interest, he or she shall be deemed to have an interest in those securities. (2) A person shall be deemed to have an interest in a security where a body corporate has an interest in a security and— (a) the body corporate is, or its directors are, accustomed or under an obligation, whether formal or informal, to act in accordance with directions, instructions or wishes of that person in relation to that security; (b) that person has a controlling interest in the body corporate; or (c) that person is, or the associates of that person or that person and his associates are, entitled to exercise or control the exercise of not less than fifteen per cent of the votes attached to the voting shares in the body corporate. (3) A person shall be deemed to have an interest in a security in any one or more of the following circumstances— (a) where he or she has entered into a contract to purchase a security; (b) where he or she has a right, otherwise than by reason of having an interest under a trust, to have a security transferred to himself or to his order, whether the right is exercisable presently or in the future and whether on the fulfillment of a condition or not; (c) where he or she has the right to acquire a security, or an interest in a security, under an option, whether on the fulfillment of a condition or not; or (d) where he or she is entitled, otherwise than by reason of his having been appointed a proxy or representative to vote at a meeting of members of a body corporate or of a class of its members, to exercise or control the exercise of a right attached to a security, not being a security of which he or she is the registered holder. (4) A person shall be deemed to have an interest in a security if that security is held jointly with another person. (5) For the purpose of determining whether a person has an interest in a security, it is immaterial that the interest cannot be related to a particular security. (6) There shall be disregarded— (a) an interest in a security if the interest is that of a person who holds the security as bare trustee; (b) an interest in a security of a person whose ordinary business includes the lending of money if he or she holds the interest only by way of security for the purposes of a transaction entered into in the ordinary course of business in connection with the lending of money; (c) an interest of a person in a security being an interest held by him or her by reason of his holding a prescribed office; and (d) a prescribed interest in a security being an interest of such person, or of the persons included in such class of persons as is prescribed. (7) An interest in a security shall not be disregarded by reason only of— (a) its remoteness; (b) the manner in which it arose; or (c) the fact that the exercise of a right conferred by the interest is, or is capable of being made, subject to restraint or restriction.

sections.heading: Establishment and membership of the Authority
sections.title: Capital Markets Authority structure and composition
sections.summary: This section establishes the Capital Markets Authority as a body corporate with perpetual succession, detailing its composition, appointment process, term of office, and removal conditions. The Authority consists of a Chairperson appointed by the President on Cabinet Secretary's recommendation, six other members appointed by the Cabinet Secretary, and specific statutory roles including the Principal Secretary to the National Treasury, Central Bank Governor, Attorney-General, and the Authority's chief executive. The section outlines the authority's legal capacity and operational framework.

requirements.text: There is hereby established an authority to be known as the Capital Markets Authority

The Authority shall be a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal and shall be capable in its corporate name of— (a) suing and being sued; (b) taking, purchasing or otherwise acquiring, holding, charging and disposing of both movable and immovable property; (c) borrowing and lending money; (d) entering into contracts; and (e) doing or performing all such other things or acts necessary for the proper performance of its functions under this Act which may lawfully be done by a body corporate

The Authority shall consist of— (a) a Chairperson to be appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Cabinet Secretary; (b) six other members appointed by the Cabinet Secretary; (c) the Principal Secretary to the National Treasury or a person deputed by him or her in writing for the purposes of this Act; (d) the Governor of the Central Bank of Kenya or a person deputed by him or her in writing for the purposes of this Act; (e) the Attorney-General or a person deputed by him or her in writing for the purposes of this Act; (f) the chief executive of the Authority

The Chairperson and every member appointed under paragraph (b) of subsection (3) shall be appointed from amongst persons who have experience and expertise in legal, financial, banking, accounting, economics or insurance matters

The Chairperson and every member appointed under paragraph (b) of subsection (3) shall hold office for a period of three years and shall be eligible for re-appointment for a further term of three years
The members of the Authority shall be appointed at different times so that the respective expiry dates of their terms of office shall fall at different times

Any member appointed under subsection (3)(b) shall cease to hold office if— (a) he or she delivers to the Cabinet Secretary a written resignation of his appointment; (b) on the advice of the Authority, the Cabinet Secretary removes him or her from office on the grounds that he or she is incapacitated by mental or physical illness or is otherwise unable or unfit to discharge the functions of a member or is unable to continue as a member; (c) he or she has been absent from three consecutive meetings of the Authority without leave or good cause; (d) he or she is adjudged bankrupt or enters into a composition scheme or arrangement with his creditors; (e) he or she is sentenced by a court to imprisonment for a term of six months or more; or (f) he or she is convicted of an offence involving dishonesty, fraud or moral turpitude

In the event of vacation of office by any member appointed under subsection (3)(b) the Cabinet Secretary may appoint another person to hold office for the unexpired period of the term of office of the member in whose place he or she is appointed

If any member of the Authority appointed under paragraph (b) of subsection (3) is temporarily unable to perform his duties, the Cabinet Secretary may appoint another person to act in his place during the period of his absence
The members of the Authority shall be paid such remuneration and allowances out of the general fund of the Authority as may be determined by the Cabinet Secretary

sections.source_quote: Capital Markets Act (Cap. 485A) Kenya 5. Establishment and membership of the Authority (1) There is hereby established an authority to be known as the Capital Markets Authority. (2) The Authority shall be a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal and shall be capable in its corporate name of— (a) suing and being sued; (b) taking, purchasing or otherwise acquiring, holding, charging and disposing of both movable and immovable property; (c) borrowing and lending money; (d) entering into contracts; and (e) doing or performing all such other things or acts necessary for the proper performance of its functions under this Act which may lawfully be done by a body corporate. (3) The Authority shall consist of— (a) a Chairperson to be appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Cabinet Secretary; (b) six other members appointed by the Cabinet Secretary; (c) the Principal Secretary to the National Treasury or a person deputed by him or her in writing for the purposes of this Act; (d) the Governor of the Central Bank of Kenya or a person deputed by him or her in writing for the purposes of this Act; (e) the Attorney-General or a person deputed by him or her in writing for the purposes of this Act; (f) the chief executive of the Authority. (4) The Chairperson and every member appointed under paragraph (b) of subsection (3) shall be appointed from amongst persons who have experience and expertise in legal, financial, banking, accounting, economics or insurance matters. (4A) The Chairperson and every member appointed under paragraph (b) of subsection (3) shall hold office for a period of three years and shall be eligible for re-appointment for a further term of three years. (4B) The members of the Authority shall be appointed at different times so that the respective expiry dates of their terms of office shall fall at different times. (5) Any member appointed under subsection (3)(b) shall cease to hold office if— (a) he or she delivers to the Cabinet Secretary a written resignation of his appointment; (b) on the advice of the Authority, the Cabinet Secretary removes him or her from office on the grounds that he or she is incapacitated by mental or physical illness or is otherwise unable or unfit to discharge the functions of a member or is unable to continue as a member; (c) he or she has been absent from three consecutive meetings of the Authority without leave or good cause; (d) he or she is adjudged bankrupt or enters into a composition scheme or arrangement with his creditors; (e) he or she is sentenced by a court to imprisonment for a term of six months or more; or (f) he or she is convicted of an offence involving dishonesty, fraud or moral turpitude. (6) In the event of vacation of office by any member appointed under subsection (3)(b) the Cabinet Secretary may appoint another person to hold office for the unexpired period of the term of office of the member in whose place he or she is appointed. (7) If any member of the Authority appointed under paragraph (b) of subsection (3) is temporarily unable to perform his duties, the Cabinet Secretary may appoint another person to act in his place during the period of his absence. (8) The members of the Authority shall be paid such remuneration and allowances out of the general fund of the Authority as may be determined by the Cabinet Secretary.

sections.heading: Meetings and procedures of the Authority
sections.title: Board meeting frequency and quorum requirements
sections.summary: The Board must meet at least six times annually with no more than two months between meetings. A quorum of six members, including the chief executive, is required for business decisions. The Chairperson presides at meetings but can be elected by members present if absent. Decisions require a majority vote, with the Chairperson having a casting vote in case of equality. Temporary appointments for the Chairperson's absence are made by the President based on Cabinet Secretary's recommendation. Resignations must be accepted by the President to take effect.
sections.dtia_summary: Board governance structure ensures timely decision-making and accountability, relevant for DTIA as it defines internal oversight mechanisms for regulatory bodies handling cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: The Board shall meet not less than six times in every financial year and not more than two months shall elapse between the date of one meeting and the date of the next meeting.
The quorum for the conduct of the business of the Board shall be six members including the chief executive.

The Chairperson shall preside at every meeting of the Board at which he or she is present but in his absence, the members present shall elect one of their number who shall, with respect to that meeting and the business transacted thereat, have all the powers of the Chairperson.

All questions for decisions at any meeting of the Authority shall be decided by the vote of the majority of the members present and in case of an equality of votes the Chairperson shall have a casting vote.

If the Chairperson of the Authority, by reason of extended illness or absence is temporarily unable to perform the duties of his office, the President, on the recommendation of the Cabinet Secretary, shall appoint another member of the Authority to act in his place during the period of absence.
The Chairperson may at any time resign by a letter addressed to the President and the resignation shall take effect upon being accepted by the President.

Any member who has a direct or indirect interest in any decision that is to be taken on any specific non-rule making matter by the Authority, shall disclose the nature of such interest at the meeting of the Authority where such decision is being taken and the disclosure shall be recorded in the minutes of the meeting, and if either the member or majority of the members of the Authority believe that such member's interest in the matter is such as to influence his judgment, he or she shall not participate in the deliberation or the decision of the Authority on such matter.

sections.source_quote: Meetings and procedures of the Authority (1) The Board shall meet not less than six times in every financial year and not more than two months shall elapse between the date of one meeting and the date of the next meeting.

sections.heading: Appointment of chief executive of the Authority
sections.title: Chief Executive appointment and qualifications
sections.summary: The Chief Executive is appointed by the Cabinet Secretary with terms of service specified in the appointment instrument. Appointees must have at least ten years of senior management experience in law, finance, accounting, economics, banking, or insurance, and expertise in money or capital markets. The Cabinet Secretary appoints the Chief Executive after consulting the Board. The Chief Executive serves four years with eligibility for reappointment for a further four years, but no more than two terms. The Chief Executive manages the Authority's affairs under the Authority's general direction.
sections.dtia_summary: Chief Executive qualifications ensure expertise in financial regulation, relevant for DTIA as it impacts the governance of entities handling cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: There shall be a Chief Executive of the Authority who shall be appointed by the Cabinet Secretary and who shall, subject to this section, hold office on such terms and conditions of service as may be specified in the instrument of appointment, or otherwise from time to time.

No person shall be qualified for appointment under this section unless such person— (a) has at least ten years' experience at a senior management level in matters relating to law, finance, accounting, economics, banking or insurance; and (b) has expertise in matters relating to money or capital markets or finance.
The Cabinet Secretary, in consultation with the Board, shall appoint a person qualified in terms of this section as the chief executive.

The chief executive shall hold office for a period of four years but shall be eligible for reappointment for a further term of four years: Provided that no person shall serve as the chief executive for more than two terms.

The chief executive shall, subject to the general direction and control of the Authority, be charged with the direction of the affairs and transactions of the Authority, the exercise, discharge and performance of its objectives, functions and duties, and the administration and control of the servants of the Authority.

sections.source_quote: Appointment of chief executive of the Authority (1) There shall be a Chief Executive of the Authority who shall be appointed by the Cabinet Secretary

sections.heading: Objectives of the Authority
sections.title: Capital Markets Authority objectives and powers
sections.summary: The Authority aims to develop capital markets, facilitate securities and derivatives markets, ensure orderly trading, protect investors, establish compensation funds, and promote electronic commerce. It may exercise powers including advising the Cabinet Secretary, implementing government policies, employing staff, imposing sanctions, granting licenses, regulating markets, and conducting inspections. The Authority also has specific powers related to electronic commerce, asset tracing, and data processing compliance with the Data Protection Act.
sections.dtia_summary: The Authority's objectives and powers include regulating data processing in capital markets, relevant for DTIA as it mandates compliance with the Data Protection Act for personal data handling.

requirements.text: The principal objectives of the Authority shall be— (a) the development of all aspects of the capital markets with particular emphasis on the removal of impediments to, and the creation of incentives for longer term investments in, productive enterprises; (b) to facilitate the existence of a nationwide system of securities commodities market and derivatives market and brokerage services so as to enable wider participation of the general public in the securities commodities market and derivatives market; (c) the creation, maintenance and regulation of a market in which securities can be issued and traded in an orderly, fair and efficient manner, through the implementation of a system in which the market participants are self-regulatory to the maximum practicable extent; (d) the protection of investor interests; (e) the facilitation of a compensation fund to protect investors from financial loss arising from the failure of a licensed broker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations; and (f) the development of a framework to facilitate the use of electronic commerce for the development of capital markets in Kenya.

For the purpose of carrying out its objectives, the Authority may exercise, perform or discharge all or any of the following powers, duties and functions— (a) advise the Cabinet Secretary on all aspects of the development and operation of capital markets; (b) implement policies and programmes of the Government with respect to the capital markets; (c) employ such officers and servants as may be necessary for the proper discharge of the functions of the Authority; (d) impose sanctions for breach of the provisions of this Act or the regulations made thereunder, or for non-compliance with the Authority's requirements or directions, and such sanctions may include— (i) levying of financial penalties, proportional to the gravity or severity of the breach, as may be prescribed; (ii) ordering a person to remedy or mitigate the effect of the breach, make restitution or pay compensation to any person aggrieved by the breach; (iii) publishing findings of malfeasance by any person; (iv) suspending or cancelling the listing of any securities or exchange-traded derivatives contracts, or the trading of any securities or exchange-traded derivatives contracts, for the protection of investors; (e) to issue guidelines and notices on all matters within the jurisdiction of the Authority under this Act; (f) to grant a licence to any person to operate as a stockbroker, derivatives broker, dealer or investment adviser, fund manager, investment bank, central depository or authorised securities dealer, and ensure the proper conduct of that business; (f) to grant approval to any person to operate as a securities exchange, commodity exchange, derivatives exchange, credit rating agency, registered venture capital company or to operate in any other capacity which directly contributes to the attainment of the objectives of this Act and to ensure the proper conduct of that business; (fa) regulate spot commodity markets; (ff) recognize any person duly licensed by a prescribed foreign authority to carry on any licensed activity in Kenya which requires a license or an approval under this Act; (g) register, approve and regulate collective investment schemes; (ga) license, approve and regulate private equity and venture capital companies that have access to public funds; (h) inquire, either on its own motion or at the request of any other person, into the affairs of any person which the Authority has approved or to which it has granted a licence and any public company the securities of which are publicly offered or traded on an approved securities exchange or on an over the counter market; (i) give directions to any person which the Authority has approved or to which it has granted a licence and any public company the securities of which are publicly offered or traded on an approved securities exchange or on an over the counter market; (j) conduct inspection of the activities, books and records of any persons approved or licensed by the Authority; (m) appoint an auditor to carry out a specific audit of the financial operations of any collective investment scheme or public company the securities of which are publicly offered or traded on an approved securities exchange or on an over the counter market, if such action is deemed to be in the interest of the investors, at the expense of such collective investment scheme or company; (n) grant compensation to any investor who suffers pecuniary loss resulting from the failure of a licensed broker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations; (o) have recourse against any person whose act or omission has resulted in a payment from the Compensation Fund; (p) act as an appellate body in respect of appeals against any self regulatory organization securities or exchange-traded derivatives contracts exchange, derivatives exchange or central depository in actions by parties aggrieved thereby; (q) co-operate or enter into agreements for mutual co-operation with other regulatory authorities for the development and regulation of cross-border activities in capital markets; (r) regulate and oversee the issue and subsequent trading, both in primary and secondary markets, of capital market instruments; (s) regulate the use of electronic commerce for dealing in securities or offer services ordinarily carried out by a licensed person; (t) trace any assets, including bank accounts, of any person who, upon investigation by the Authority, is found to have engaged in any fraudulent dealings in an issuer and its securities or insider trading; (u) in writing, order caveats to be placed against the title to such assets or prohibit any such person from operating any such bank accounts as may be directed by the Authority, pending determination of any charges instituted against that person; (v) prescribe notices or guidelines on corporate governance of a company whose securities have been issued to the public or a section of the public; (va) ensure processing of personal data in the operations of capital markets is in accordance with principles set out under the Data Protection Act (Cap. 411C); (w) do all such other acts as may be incidental or conducive to the attainment of the objectives of the Authority or the exercise of its powers under this Act.

sections.source_quote: Objectives of the Authority (1) The principal objectives of the Authority shall be— (a) the development of all aspects of the capital markets with particular emphasis on the removal of impediments to, and the creation of incentives for longer term investments in, productive enterprises; (b) to facilitate the existence of a nationwide system of securities commodities market and derivatives market and brokerage services so as to enable wider participation of the general public in the securities commodities market and derivatives market; (c) the creation, maintenance and regulation of a market in which securities can be issued and traded in an orderly, fair and efficient manner, through the implementation of a system in which the market participants are self-regulatory to the maximum practicable extent; (d) the protection of investor interests; (e) the facilitation of a compensation fund to protect investors from financial loss arising from the failure of a licensed broker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations; and (f) the development of a framework to facilitate the use of electronic commerce for the development of capital markets in Kenya.

sections.heading: Power of the Cabinet Secretary to issue rules and regulations
sections.title: Cabinet Secretary's power to issue capital markets rules
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary may issue rules and regulations to govern listing and de-listing of securities, disclosures by market participants, record-keeping requirements, and other capital market activities. These rules must align with promoting an efficient securities market and be publicly exposed for 30 days for stakeholder feedback. Stakeholders include listed companies and entities licensed by the Authority with operations affecting capital markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Rules require public consultation before implementation and define stakeholders for capital markets regulation, impacting how data transfers and processing are governed within Kenya's regulatory framework.

requirements.text: The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate rules and regulations to regulate listing and de-listing of securities on a securities exchange
The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate rules and regulations to regulate disclosures about securities transactions by stockbrokers, derivatives brokers and dealers
The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate rules and regulations to regulate the keeping and proper maintenance of books, records, accounts and audits by all persons approved or licensed by the Authority
All rules and regulations formulated under subsection (1) shall take into account and be consistent with the objective of promoting and maintaining an effective and efficient securities market

All rules and regulations formulated under subsection (1) shall be exposed for comment by stakeholders and the general public for a period of thirty days through notification in at least two daily newspapers of national circulation and the electronic media

Stakeholders shall include listed companies and all persons licensed or approved by the Authority or financial or other institutions whose operations have, in the opinion of the Authority, a bearing on the development and regulation of capital markets in Kenya

sections.source_quote: The Cabinet Secretary shall formulate such rules and regulations as may be required to regulate— (a) listing and de-listing of securities on a securities exchange; (b) disclosures about securities transactions by— (i) stockbrokers, derivatives brokers and dealers; (ii) persons who acquire or dispose of securities or exchange-traded derivatives contracts; (iii) a securities exchange;

sections.heading: Furnishing of information to the Authority
sections.title: Information disclosure to the Authority
sections.summary: The Authority may require persons to provide information in writing within a specified period. The Authority may not disclose information to third parties except for its objectives unless required by court. The Authority may share information with foreign regulatory bodies under specific conditions, including cost-sharing and reciprocal assistance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs data sharing with regulatory authorities, impacting cross-border data transfers and compliance with international regulatory requests.

requirements.text: The Authority or any person officially authorized in that behalf by the Authority may, by notice in writing, require any person to furnish to the Authority or to the authorized person, within such period as is specified in the notice, all such returns or information as specified in such notice

The Authority or any member thereof, or any officer or servant of the Authority, shall not disclose to any person or use any return or information acquired under subsection (1) except for the purpose of achieving the objectives of the Authority unless required to do so by a court of law

The Authority may, where it receives a request from a regulatory body, whether established within or outside Kenya, for assistance in investigating a person specified by the regulatory body who has contravened or is contravening any legal or regulatory requirements which— (a) are enforced or administers by that regulatory body; or (b) relate to securities transactions regulated by that regulatory body
The Authority may provide the assistance requested for by exercising any of its powers under this Act or by providing such other assistance as the Authority may consider necessary
A regulatory body which requests for assistance under subsection (3) shall demonstrate that it is desirable or expedient that the assistance requested should be provided in the interest of the public
A regulatory body which requests for assistance under subsection (3) shall demonstrate that the assistance shall assist the regulatory body in the discharge and performance of its functions

The Authority shall, in deciding whether the requirement under subsection (5) is satisfied in a particular case, take into account whether the regulatory body shall pay the Authority any of the costs and expenses incurred in providing the assistance

The Authority shall, in deciding whether the requirement under subsection (5) is satisfied in a particular case, take into account whether the regulatory body shall be able and willing to provide reciprocal assistance within its jurisdiction in response to a similar request for assistance from Kenya

sections.source_quote: The Authority or any person officially authorized in that behalf by the Authority may, by notice in writing, require any person to furnish to the Authority or to the authorized person, within such period as is specified in the notice, all such returns or information as specified in such notice.

sections.heading: Financial year of Authority
sections.title: Financial year for Capital Markets Authority
sections.summary: This section defines the financial year for the Capital Markets Authority as twelve months starting on July 1st of each year. The Authority must operate within this fiscal period for accounting and reporting purposes. The provision applies to the Authority's internal financial management and reporting obligations.

requirements.text: The financial year of the Authority shall be the period of twelve months commencing on the first day of July in each year.

sections.source_quote: 16. Financial year of Authority The financial year of the Authority shall be the period of twelve months commencing on the first day of July in each year.

sections.heading: Accounts
sections.title: Account-keeping requirements for Authority
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Authority maintain proper books of accounts covering its income, expenditures, assets, liabilities, and all other transactions. The requirement applies to the Authority's financial record-keeping and reporting obligations. It ensures transparency and accountability in the Authority's financial operations.

requirements.text: The Authority shall cause proper books of accounts to be kept of its income and expenditures, assets and liabilities and all other transactions of the Authority.

sections.source_quote: 17. Accounts The Authority shall cause proper books of accounts to be kept of its income and expenditures, assets and liabilities and all other transactions of the Authority.

sections.heading: Establishment of the Investor Compensation Fund
sections.title: Investor Compensation Fund establishment for stockbroker failures
sections.summary: This section mandates the creation of an Investor Compensation Fund to compensate investors who suffer financial loss due to licensed stockbrokers or dealers failing to meet contractual obligations. The Fund is funded through contributions from licensed persons, fines, penalties, interest earnings, recovered sums, and approved external sources. The Authority may reward individuals who provide timely information leading to Fund recovery, with rewards capped at five million shillings. Offenders colluding with the Authority to collect rewards face fines up to five million shillings or imprisonment for five years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a fund for investor compensation but does not directly impact data transfers. It is irrelevant to Data Transfer Impact Assessments as it concerns financial compensation mechanisms rather than data handling.

requirements.text: There shall be established a Fund to be known as the Investor Compensation Fund for the purposes of granting compensation to investors who suffer pecuniary loss resulting from the failure of a licensed stockbroker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations.

The Compensation Fund shall consist of— (a) such moneys as are required to be paid into the Compensation Fund by licensed persons; (b) such sums of money as are paid under this Act as fines or penalties or under section 34 as ill-gotten gains where those harmed are not specifically identifiable; (c) such sums of money as accrue from interest and profits from investing Compensation Fund moneys; (d) such sums of money recovered by or on behalf of the Authority from entities whose failure to meet their obligations to investors result in payments from the Compensation Fund; (e) interest deemed to accrue on the proceeds of a public issue or offer for sale of shares of a company listed or to be listed on an approved securities exchange, between the closing date and the date of dispatch of refund cheques, or, where there is no refund, the date of dispatch of share certificates or crediting of securities accounts, to be determined at the rate prescribed by the Authority; (f) such sums of money as are received for purposes of the Compensation Fund from any other source approved by the Cabinet Secretary.

The Authority may reward any person who provides new and timely information leading to the recovery of sums of money referred to in subsection (2): Provided that— (a) this provision shall not apply to any officer of the Authority; (b) the reward payable under this subsection shall be three per cent of the amount recovered subject to a maximum of five million shillings; and (c) the reward referred to in paragraph (a) shall be paid before the recovered sums of money are transferred to the Fund.

A person who— (a) colludes with an officer of the Authority for the purpose of collecting the reward under subsection (2A); (b) while working at the Authority aids another person to get and provide information under subsection (2A); (c) provides false information under subsection (2A), commits an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years.

sections.source_quote: 18. Establishment of the Investor Compensation Fund (1) There shall be established a Fund to be known as the Investor Compensation Fund for the purposes of granting compensation to investors who suffer pecuniary loss resulting from the failure of a licensed stockbroker or dealer to meet his contractual obligations.

sections.heading: Establishment of the Investor Compensation Fund (cont.)
sections.title: Self-regulatory organization officer appointment review
sections.summary: Self-regulatory organizations must not appoint or retain officers who could harm the organization or investor interests without first providing the officer and organization a chance to respond. The authority may direct the organization to remove such officers. This applies to all self-regulatory organizations in Kenya under the Capital Markets Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires self-regulatory organizations to assess officer appointments for potential harm to investors before implementation, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial services regulators.

requirements.text: An appointment of a person or the continuing in office as an officer of a self-regulatory organization is likely to be detrimental to the self-regulatory organization, or may prejudice the interest of investors and consumers of financial services or members of the relevant sector or industry
After giving the officer and the self-regulatory organization an opportunity to be heard, direct the self-regulatory organization not to appoint the officer, or to remove the officer from office

sections.source_quote: an appointment of a person or the continuing in office as an officer of a self-regulatory organization is likely to be detrimental to the self-regulatory organization, or may prejudice the interest of investors and consumers of financial services or members of the relevant sector or industry, after giving the officer and the self-regulatory organization an opportunity to be heard, direct the self-regulatory organization not to appoint the officer, or to remove the officer from office.

sections.heading: Application for securities exchange, commodities exchange approval
sections.title: Application for securities exchange approval
sections.summary: An entity seeking approval to operate as a securities exchange, commodities exchange, or derivatives exchange must submit an application to the Authority in the prescribed form and manner along with the required fee. The Authority may approve the applicant if it satisfies that the applicant is a limited liability company, has a properly constituted board of directors, and has adopted rules compliant with the Act and regulations. Approved exchanges must comply with all Authority requirements and pay an annual fee. The Authority may also require the applicant to obtain self-regulatory organization recognition as a condition for licensing.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for obtaining exchange approval, including the need for compliant rules and annual fees, which may impact data transfer considerations for exchanges handling financial data.

requirements.text: An application for securities exchange, commodities exchange or derivatives exchange approval shall be made to the Authority in the form and manner prescribed by the Authority and shall be accompanied by the prescribed fee.

The Authority may approve a person as a securities exchange, commodities exchange or derivatives exchange if it is satisfied that the applicant is a limited liability company whose liability is limited by shares, or as may be prescribed by the Authority.

The Authority may approve a person as a securities exchange, commodities exchange or derivatives exchange if it is satisfied that the applicant's board of directors is constituted in a manner prescribed by the Authority.

The Authority may approve a person as a securities exchange, commodities exchange or derivatives exchange if it is satisfied that the applicant has made and adopted rules in compliance with the Act and any Regulations made thereunder.

All fees to be charged by a securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange shall be subject to prior approval by the Authority notwithstanding the constitution of such securities exchange.

An approved securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange shall comply with all requirements of the Authority and pay an annual fee to the Authority at such rate as the Authority may prescribe.

The Authority may require an applicant for a licence as a securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange to lodge an application to be recognized as a self regulatory organization as a condition for obtaining and maintaining its licence.

sections.source_quote: 20. Application for securities exchange, commodities exchange approval (1) An application for securities exchange, commodities exchange or derivatives exchange approval shall be made to the Authority in the form and manner prescribed by the Authority and shall be accompanied by the prescribed fee.

sections.heading: Changes in securities exchange, commodities exchange rules
sections.title: Changes to exchange rules
sections.summary: Approved exchanges must seek prior Authority approval before amending their rules, which are subject to review within 30 days. The Authority may allow rule changes that are policy statements, fee adjustments, or administrative matters without full approval, but must provide reasons for disallowance. Exchanges must inform the Authority of any changes to previously submitted information before implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulates rule changes for exchanges, which may affect data transfer if exchanges handle financial data across borders.

requirements.text: The rules of an approved securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange, in so far as they have been approved by the Authority, shall not be amended, varied or rescinded without the prior approval of the Authority.

Where the board of directors of an approved securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange wishes to amend its rules, it shall forward the amendments to the Authority for approval.

The Authority shall, after hearing from the securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange, and within thirty days of receipt of a notice under subsection (2) give written notice to the securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange stating whether such amendments to the rules are allowed or disallowed and in the event of the rules being disallowed, the Authority shall give reasons for such disallowance.

Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraph (2), a proposed rule change may take effect upon filing with the Authority if designated by the exchanges as a stated policy, practice or interpretation with respect to the meaning, administration or enforcement of an existing rule.

Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraph (2), a proposed rule change may take effect upon filing with the Authority if designated by the exchanges as a proposal establishing or changing a fee or other charge.

Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraph (2), a proposed rule change may take effect upon filing with the Authority if designated by the exchanges as a proposal dealing solely with the administration of the exchange or other matters which the Authority may specify.

Where an approved securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange proposes to alter any particulars already furnished or undergoes or intends to undergo a change from its state specified in the application for approval it shall inform the Authority and obtain its prior consent before such alteration or change is effected.

sections.source_quote: 21. Changes in securities exchange, commodities exchange rules (1) The rules of an approved securities exchange, commodities exchange or a derivatives exchange, in so far as they have been approved by the Authority, shall not be amended, varied or rescinded without the prior approval of the Authority.

sections.heading: Disciplinary action by securities exchange
sections.title: Disciplinary action by securities exchanges
sections.summary: Securities exchanges must notify the Authority within seven days of taking disciplinary action against trading participants or listed companies. The Authority may review such actions, affirm or set aside decisions after providing opportunities for hearing. The Authority can also take disciplinary action itself if an exchange fails to act, but must give prior notice and opportunity to be heard.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires exchanges to report disciplinary actions to the regulator; regulator can override exchange decisions; regulator may directly discipline participants if exchanges fail to act.

requirements.text: Securities exchanges must notify the Authority within seven days of taking disciplinary action against trading participants or listed companies.
The Authority may review disciplinary actions taken by exchanges and affirm or set aside decisions after providing opportunities for hearing.
The Authority can take disciplinary action itself if an exchange fails to act, but must give prior notice and opportunity to be heard.

sections.source_quote: Where a securities exchange or a derivatives exchange reprimands, fines, suspends or expels, or otherwise takes disciplinary action against a trading participant or a listed company, it shall within seven days give notice to the Authority in writing, giving particulars including the name of the person, the reason for and nature of the action taken.

sections.heading: Licences required
sections.title: Licences required for financial activities
sections.summary: Individuals and entities must hold valid licenses from the Authority to operate as stockbrokers, dealers, fund managers, or other financial intermediaries. Exchanges and other entities must be approved by the Authority. License holders must comply with requirements and pay annual fees.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulator requires licenses for financial activities; entities must be approved to operate; license holders must pay annual fees.

requirements.text: Individuals and entities must hold valid licenses from the Authority to operate as stockbrokers, derivatives brokers, fund managers, or other financial intermediaries.
Exchanges and other entities must be approved by the Authority.
License holders must comply with Authority requirements and pay annual fees.

sections.source_quote: No person shall carry on business as a stockbroker, derivatives broker, REIT manager, trustee, dealer, investment adviser, fund manager, investment bank, central depository, authorised securities dealer, authorized depository, online forex broker, commodity dealer, commodity broker or hold himself out as carrying on such a business unless he or she holds a valid licence issued under this Act or under the authority of this Act.

sections.heading: Application for licence
sections.title: Application for licence
sections.summary: Applications for licenses must be submitted to the Authority with prescribed forms and fees. The Authority may require additional information, assess financial requirements, impose conditions, and provide opportunities for hearing before granting licenses.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulator requires applications for licenses with specific forms and fees; assesses financial requirements; imposes conditions; provides hearing opportunities.

requirements.text: Applications for licenses must be submitted to the Authority with prescribed forms and fees.
The Authority may require additional information on applications.
The Authority may assess financial requirements before granting licenses.
The Authority may impose conditions or restrictions on licenses.
The Authority must provide opportunities for hearing before granting licenses.

sections.source_quote: An application for a licence or for the renewal of a licence shall be made to the Authority in the prescribed form and shall be accompanied by the prescribed fee and in the case of an application for the renewal of a licence, may be made within three months but not later than one month prior to the expiry of the licence.

sections.heading: [Repealed by , s. 18.]
sections.title: Imposition of sanctions and penalties
sections.summary: The Authority may impose sanctions including public reprimands, suspensions, restrictions on licenses, recovery of benefits, financial penalties, and restitution orders for breaches of the Act. Penalties vary by role and may be doubled for certain violations.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulator can impose sanctions including suspensions, financial penalties, and restitution orders for breaches; penalties may be doubled for certain violations.

requirements.text: The Authority may impose public reprimands, suspensions, restrictions on licenses, recovery of benefits, and financial penalties for breaches of the Act.
Financial penalties for licensed persons may not exceed ten million shillings.
Financial penalties for employees may not exceed five million shillings.
The Authority may order restitution for losses caused by breaches of the Act.
Penalties for trading rule breaches may be doubled.
Penalties for reporting failures may be doubled.

sections.source_quote: Without prejudice to any other provision of this Act, the Authority may impose the following sanctions or levy financial penalties in accordance with this Act, for the breach of any provisions of this Act, the regulations, rules, guidelines, notices or directions made thereunder, or the rules of procedure of a securities, commodities or derivatives exchange, by a licensed or approved person, issuer, employee or a director of a licensed or approved person or employee director of a issuer as provided under section 11(3)(cc)

sections.heading: Suspension or revocation of a licence
sections.title: Conditions for suspending or revoking a license
sections.summary: The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who goes into liquidation, engages in unauthorized activities, has a receiver appointed, ceases business without approval, has directors not performing duties honestly, fails to comply with license conditions, ignores Authority directions, provides false information, or is no longer fit to hold a license. Suspension cannot exceed three months and may be extended once. Revocation occurs if annual fees are unpaid within thirty days of suspension. The Authority must publish suspended or revoked licenses in the Gazette and provide affected persons an opportunity to be heard.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions under which a license can be suspended or revoked, impacting data transfer by requiring licensed entities to maintain compliance with regulatory obligations to avoid loss of authorization for cross-border data processing activities.

requirements.text: The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who goes into liquidation or has an order issued for winding up
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who carries out activities outside the scope of licensed or approved activities
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who has a receiver or manager appointed on all or substantial part of the company's property
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who ceases business without approval for more than thirty days
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person whose directors or key employees have not performed duties honestly and fairly
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who has contravened license conditions
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who fails to comply with Authority directions
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who fails to provide required information
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who provides false or misleading information
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who is no longer fit to hold a license
The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for a licensed person who breaches other provisions of the Act
Suspension of a license may not exceed three months and may be extended once
The Authority must lift suspension or revoke license at end of suspension period
Failure to pay annual fee results in license suspension
If suspension occurs and fee not paid within thirty days, license is revoked
The Authority may revoke or suspend a license at request of licensed person
The Authority must publish suspended or revoked licenses in Gazette
The Authority must provide affected persons opportunity to be heard

sections.source_quote: The Authority may suspend or revoke a license for such period or until the occurrence of such event as the Authority may specify, if a licensed person—(a) goes into liquidation or an order is issued for the winding up of the licensed person; (b) carries out any activity outside the scope of the licensed or approved activities; (c) has a receiver or a manager appointed on all or a substantial part of the property of the company; (d) ceases to carry on the licensed business for a period of more than thirty days unless it has obtained the approval of the Authority to do so; (e) any of its directors or key employees has not, in the opinion of the Authority, performed their duties honestly and fairly; (f) has contravened or failed to comply with any condition applicable in respect of the licence; (g) fails to comply with a direction of the Authority; (h) fails to provide the Authority with such information as it may require; (i) provides false or misleading information; (j) for any other reason, is no longer fit and proper person to hold a license; or (k) is in breach of any other provision under this Act.

sections.heading: Obligation to report changes
sections.title: Reporting changes to license registration
sections.summary: A licensed person must report to the Authority within fourteen days if they cease carrying on licensed business or if there are changes to particulars required to be entered in the license holder's register. This ensures the Authority maintains up-to-date information about licensed entities for regulatory oversight purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely reporting of changes to license registration, which impacts data transfer by ensuring licensed entities maintain accurate records of their business activities and data handling practices.

requirements.text: A licensed person must report to the Authority within fourteen days if they cease carrying on business to which the license relates
A licensed person must report to the Authority within fourteen days if there are changes to particulars required to be entered in the license holder's register

sections.source_quote: Where—(a) the holder of a licence ceases to carry on the business to which the licence relates; or (b) a change occurs in any particulars which are required by section 27 to be entered in a register of licence holders with respect to the holder of a licence, the holder of the licence shall within fourteen days of the occurrence of the event concerned, give to the Authority, particulars of such event.

sections.heading: Licensing requirements
sections.title: Licensing requirements for financial entities
