sections.summary: Before granting a license, the Authority must verify that applicants are legally constituted entities, have qualified directors, meet specific security requirements for stockbrokers, have administrative capacity, operate solely for clients (stockbrokers), or for their own benefit (dealers), and comply with shareholding limits to prevent undue control. The Authority also ensures that securities exchanges admit applicants who meet all requirements and pay admission fees.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes licensing requirements that impact data transfer by mandating specific operational and structural conditions for financial entities handling data, including shareholding limits and operational restrictions.

requirements.text: The Authority must verify that applicants are legally constituted entities or duly constituted collective investment schemes
The Authority must verify that at least one director, CEO, or person directing the business has satisfied minimum qualification requirements
The Authority must verify that stockbrokers have lodged security or equivalent bank guarantee with the securities exchange or Authority
The Authority must verify that applicants have necessary administrative capacity to carry on business
The Authority must verify that stockbrokers operate solely on behalf of clients
The Authority must verify that dealers operate solely on their own behalf
The Authority must verify that derivatives brokers may operate on behalf of clients or for their own benefit
The Authority must ensure securities exchanges admit applicants who fulfill all requirements and pay admission fees
The Authority must ensure that stockbrokers, dealers, or derivatives brokers cease to be trading participants if their license is revoked
The Authority must ensure that no person controls more than 33.33% of a company's shares, voting rights, or dividends
The Authority must ensure that no person appoints more than one-third of a company's board members
The Authority must ensure that no person receives more than 33.33% of aggregate dividends and interest on shareholder loans

sections.source_quote: Before granting any licence or approval, the Authority in respect of a business that requires to be licensed or approved shall satisfy itself—(a) that the applicant is such legal entity as may be prescribed in the Regulations as the Authority may prescribe or is duly constituted as a collective investment scheme; (b) that at least one the director, chief executive officer or such other person who directs, conducts, manages or supervises the business of the applicant has satisfied such minimum qualification requirements as may be prescribed; (c) in the case of a stockbroker, dealer or other person prescribed by the Authority that the applicant company has lodged security in such sum as may be determined by the Authority or an equivalent bank guarantee or bond with the securities exchange in which it is a trading participant or with the Authority or other person approved by the Authority as the case may be; (d) that the applicant company has the necessary administrative capacity to carry on business for which the licence is required; (e) in the case of an application for a stockbroker's licence, that the applicant shall carry on business solely on behalf of clients; (f) in the case of an application for a dealer's licence, that the applicant shall carry on business solely on the applicant's own behalf; (gg) in the case of an application for a derivatives broker licences, that the applicant may carry on business either on behalf of clients or on the applicant's own behalf, or both; (h) deleted by , s. 7.

sections.heading: Procedure for collective investment schemes
sections.title: Collective investment schemes and securities offers
sections.summary: Collective investment schemes must be registered with the Authority before operating in Kenya. Promoters must apply for consent to register within three months of receiving consent, providing proof of legal constitution, incorporation documents, and registration applications. The Authority registers schemes that comply with requirements and issues certificates of registration. Schemes must publish information memoranda before offering shares publicly, and must comply with disclosure obligations to ensure transparency in securities transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the registration and operation of collective investment schemes, which impacts data transfer by requiring detailed registration and disclosure processes for entities handling financial data across borders.

requirements.text: No person may carry on business as a collective investment scheme in or from within Kenya without registration
Promoters must apply for consent to register a collective investment scheme upon complying with requirements
Promoters must deliver satisfactory proof of legal constitution within three months of consent
Promoters must submit proposed incorporation documents and other information as required by the Authority
Promoters must submit an application for registration in prescribed form with prescribed fee
The Authority registers collective investment schemes that comply with requirements and issues certificate of registration
A mutual fund deemed to be incorporated as a collective investment scheme with variable capital upon registration
Investment companies may operate without registration for six months from commencement of the Act
Investment companies must apply for registration before expiration of six-month period
Registered collective investment schemes may not offer shares publicly without publishing information memorandum
Information memoranda must comply with Authority requirements
Issuers must keep Authority, members, other holders, listing exchanges, and public informed of material information

sections.source_quote: No person shall carry on any business or engage in any activity as a collective investment scheme, in or from within Kenya, unless such person is registered under this Act. The promoters of a collective investment scheme that is proposed to be formed, may apply to the Authority for consent to register a collective investment scheme upon complying with the requirements prescribed under this Act.

sections.heading: Procedure for collective investment schemes (cont.)

sections.heading: Procedure for collective investment schemes (cont.)
sections.title: Obligations for restricted offers in securitisation transactions
sections.summary: This section mandates that false statements, misleading omissions, or deceptive representations in prospectuses or applications for asset-backed securities or securitisation transactions must include all statements in prospectuses, offering memoranda, transaction documents, and credit rating agency communications. It also requires that continuing disclosure obligations apply to these transactions. The section specifies that provisions from sections 30D, 30E, 30F, 30JM, 30K, and 33O apply to restricted offers involving asset-backed securities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes specific disclosure and verification requirements for restricted offers, which are critical for ensuring transparency and compliance in cross-border securitisation transactions involving asset-backed securities.

requirements.text: A reference to a false, untrue, misleading or deceptive statement in a prospectus or omission of information or a statement when making an application relating to or in connection with any securitisation transaction or issue or offer of asset backed securities shall include any statement made in or any representation or warranty included in any prospectus or offering memorandum, transaction document or made to any credit rating agency in connection with the rating of any asset backed securities or the review of such a rating and includes any omission.
The provisions of section 30D, 30E and 30F shall apply to an application relating to or in connection with a securitization transaction or issue or offer of asset backed securities under this Part.

The provisions of section 30D, 30E, 30JM, 30K and 33O shall apply to an offering memorandum in relation to an issue or offer involving a restricted offer, whether it is made in combination with or is a tranche of another issue or offer.

sections.source_quote: Obligations applicable to restricted offers (1) A reference in this Act to a false, untrue, misleading or deceptive statement in a prospectus or to the omission of information or a statement when making an application relating to or in connection with any securitisation transaction or any issue or offer of asset backed securities shall— (a) include any statement made in or any representation or warranty included in any prospectus or offering memorandum, transaction document or made to any credit rating agency in connection with the rating of any asset backed securities or the review of such a rating and includes any omission; (b) include any information, statement in, or, omission from, the continuing disclosure obligations under this Act.

sections.heading: Transactions in securities
sections.title: Securities transfers outside exchange: authorization requirements
sections.summary: Licensed persons, brokers, and dealers must obtain explicit authorization from the Capital Markets Authority (CMA) to transfer listed securities outside the exchange where they are a trading participant. This authorization requires payment of a prescribed fee and is granted under specific conditions: private transactions, over-the-counter trading with reporting, or in the interest of ordinary shareholders. The CMA may also prescribe rules for clearance, settlement, payment, and transfer of securities to prevent contraventions. Transactions in margin accounts must comply with CMA requirements, and no person may lend securities without customer consent or engage in manipulative deception or fraud.
sections.dtia_summary: Transfers of listed securities outside the exchange require CMA authorization, which impacts cross-border data transfers involving securities. The authorization conditions (private transactions, over-the-counter trading, shareholder interests) and fee payment create specific safeguards for data transfers across borders.

requirements.text: No licensed person, broker or dealer shall transfer listed securities outside the securities exchange in which he or she is a trading participant except as provided for by the Authority in rules or as authorised by the Authority on a case by case basis, and on payment of a prescribed fee.

The Authority may authorise the transfer of a listed security outside the securities exchange if the Authority is satisfied that the transaction is a private transaction as prescribed by the Authority; or the security trades over the counter and such trade is reported in accordance with the rules prescribed by the Authority; or it would be in the interest of the holders of ordinary shares of the company having regard to the prevailing conditions and all factors which are relevant in the circumstances to so authorise.

No licensed person, broker or dealer shall trade in listed securities in contravention of such rules as the Authority shall prescribe with respect to the clearance, settlement, payment, transfer or delivery of securities.
No licensed person, broker or dealer shall effect any transaction in a margin account in a manner contrary to requirements adopted by the Authority.

No licensed person, broker or dealer shall lend or arrange for the lending of any securities carried for the account of any customer without the customer's written consent, or borrow, or arrange to borrow, using the securities, carried for the account of any customer, as collateral, without the customer's written consent.

No licensed person, broker or dealer shall effect any transaction in, or induce or attempt to induce the purchase or sale of, any listed security by means of any manipulative deception, or other fraudulent device or contrivance.

No person holding shares in a public company listed on an approved securities exchange, shall sell or transfer such shares except in compliance with the trading procedures adopted by such securities exchange.

No person shall, directly or indirectly, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security— employ any device, scheme or artifice to defraud; engage in any act, practice or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person; make any untrue statement of a material fact; or omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made in light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading.

sections.source_quote: 31. Transactions in securities (1) No licensed person, broker or dealer shall transfer listed securities outside the securities exchange in which he or she is a trading participant except as provided for by the Authority in rules or as authorised by the Authority on a case by case basis, and on payment of a prescribed fee.

sections.heading: Register of interest in securities
sections.title: Registration of securities interests and insider trading prohibitions
sections.summary: This section mandates that licensed persons and financial journalists maintain a register of securities interests within seven days of acquisition or change. The Authority may inspect this register. It also defines insider trading, inside information, and market manipulation offenses, including penalties for contravening these rules. The section specifies that insider information must relate to securities, not be public, and likely to affect prices. It prohibits fraudulent activities, false statements, and market manipulation to prevent insider trading and ensure transparency in securities transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: The registration requirements and insider trading prohibitions impact cross-border data transfers involving securities by requiring transparency and authorization for transfers, which are critical for DTIA compliance in financial data contexts.

requirements.text: A person to whom section (1) applies shall maintain a register of the securities in which he or she has an interest and such interest or any changes in such interest shall be entered in the register within seven days of the acquisition or change in the interest.

The Authority or any person authorized by it in that behalf may require any person to whom section (1) applies to produce for inspection the register required under subsection (4) and the Authority or any person so authorized may make extracts from the register.

A person who deals in listed securities or their derivatives that are price-affected in relation to the information in his possession commits an offence of insider trading if that person encourages another person, whether or not that other person knows it, to deal in securities or their derivatives which are price-affected securities in relation to the information in the possession of the insider, knowing or having reasonable cause to believe that the trading would take place; or discloses the information, otherwise than in the proper performance of the functions of his employment, office or profession, to another person.

For the purposes of this Part, 'inside information' means information which relates to particular securities or to a particular issuer of securities; has not been made public; and if it were made public is likely to have a material effect on the price of the securities.

A person who enters into or carries out, directly or indirectly, two or more transactions in the securities of a company, or in other listed securities, which by themselves or in conjunction with any other transaction increase, reduce, or stabilize the price with the intention of inducing another person to purchase, sell, or refrain from purchasing or selling securities, commits an offence.

sections.source_quote: 32. Register of interest in securities (4) A person to whom section (1) applies shall maintain a register of the securities in which he or she has an interest and such interest or any changes in such interest shall be entered in the register within seven days of the acquisition or change in the interest.

sections.heading: Register of interest in securities (cont.)
sections.title: Fraudulent gain and insider trading penalties
sections.summary: This section establishes offences for obtaining financial or personal gain through fraud, deceit, or intentional concealment. It prohibits fraudulent means to gain from the public, issuers, or regulated persons. The penalties include fines up to five million shillings for individuals or ten million shillings for companies, plus payment of twice the gain made or loss avoided.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes penalties for fraudulent activities that could impact data transfers involving financial institutions, requiring robust safeguards to prevent misuse of sensitive financial information.

requirements.text: Any person who on his own action or conspires with another by deceit, intentional concealment, omission or any fraudulent means to obtain financial or personal gain from the public, an issuer or a regulated person commits an offence.

A person who contravenes the provisions of this Part commits an offence and is liable on conviction in the case of—(a) an individual, to a fine not exceeding five million shillings or to imprisonment for a term of two years and payment of twice the amount of the gain made or loss avoided; (b) company, to a fine not exceeding ten million shillings and payment of twice the amount of the gain made or loss avoided.

sections.source_quote: Any person who on his own action or conspires with another by deceit, intentional concealment, omission or any fraudulent means to obtain financial or personal gain from the public, an issuer or a regulated person commits an offence.

sections.heading: Other offences
sections.title: Other offences
sections.summary: This section defines additional offences under the Capital Markets Act, including contravening provisions of the Act, furnishing false information, or obstructing the Authority's duties. These offences carry penalties including fines and imprisonment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it addresses offences related to financial data handling, requiring robust compliance mechanisms to prevent fraudulent data transfers.

requirements.text: Any person who contravenes any provision of this Act or any requirement imposed under the provision of this Act or any rule or regulation made thereunder commits an offence.

Any person who furnishes or publishes for the purpose of this Act or in connection with an issuer whose securities are listed or quoted to be listed on a securities exchange, or issued or to be issued to the public or a collective investment scheme, any information or any returns the contents of which are to his knowledge untrue or incorrect or misleading because of material omissions shall be guilty of an offence.

Any person who wilfully obstructs any member of the Authority or an officer or servant of the Authority in the performance of his duties under the provisions of that Act shall be guilty of an offence.

sections.source_quote: Any person who—(a) contravenes any provision of this Act or any requirement imposed under the provision of this Act or any rule or regulation made thereunder; (b) furnishes or publishes for the purpose of this Act or in connection with an issuer whose securities are listed or quoted to be listed on a securities exchange, or issued or to be issued to the public or a collective investment scheme, any information or any returns the contents of which are to his knowledge untrue or incorrect or misleading because of material omissions; or (c) wilfully obstructs any member of the Authority or an officer or servant of the Authority in the performance of his duties under the provisions of that Act, shall be guilty of an offence.

sections.heading: Appeals from action by Authority
sections.title: Appeals from Authority decisions on securities licenses and compensation
sections.summary: Any person aggrieved by an Authority decision regarding license refusal, restrictions, suspension, revocation, public offer approval, securities listing, or compensation can appeal to the Capital Markets Tribunal within fifteen days. The Tribunal may require the Authority to show cause, affirm or set aside decisions after hearing, and award costs. The Tribunal has powers similar to the High Court including summoning witnesses, taking evidence, and awarding costs. Appeals must be filed within ninety days, and the High Court may further review decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for challenging Authority decisions on securities licenses and compensation, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border disputes with Kenyan financial regulators.

requirements.text: Any person aggrieved by any direction given by the Authority to such person or by a decision of the Authority or by the Investor Compensation Fund Board refusing to grant a licence, imposing limitations or restrictions on a licence, suspending or revoking a licence, refusing to approve a public offer of securities, refusing to admit a security to the official list of a securities exchange, suspending trading of a security on a securities exchange, or requiring the removal of a security from the official list of a securities exchange, may appeal to the Capital Markets Tribunal against such directions, refusal, limitations or restrictions, cancellations, suspension or removal, as the case may be, within fifteen days from the date on which the decision was communicated to such person.

The Capital Markets Tribunal may require the Authority or the Investor Compensation Fund Board to show cause for its action or decision, and may affirm or, after affording the Authority or the Board an opportunity to be heard, set aside such action or decision.

The Tribunal shall, upon an appeal made to it in writing by an aggrieved party following a determination by the Authority on any matter relating to this Act, inquire into the matter and make an award thereon, and every award made shall be notified by the Tribunal to the parties concerned and the Authority as the case may be.

For the purposes of hearing an appeal, the Tribunal shall have all the powers of the High Court to summon witnesses, to take evidence upon oath or affirmation and to call for the production of books and other documents.
The Tribunal shall hear and determine an appeal within ninety days from the date of filing of the appeal.

Any party to proceedings before the Tribunal who is dissatisfied by a decision or order of the Tribunal on a point of law may, within thirty days of the decision or order, appeal against such decision or order to the High Court.

sections.source_quote: Any person aggrieved by any direction given by the Authority to such person or by a decision of the Authority or by the Investor Compensation Fund Board—refusing to grant a licence; imposing limitations or restrictions on a licence; suspending or revoking a licence; refusing to approve a public offer of securities; refusing to admit a security to the official list of a securities exchange; suspending trading of a security on a securities exchange; or requiring the removal of a security from the official list of a securities exchange—may appeal to the Capital Markets Tribunal against such directions, refusal, limitations or restrictions, cancellations, suspension or removal, as the case may be, within fifteen days from the date on which the decision was communicated to such person.

sections.heading: Directions and submission of reports
sections.title: Reporting requirements for financial authorities
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary may direct the Authority to provide returns, accounts, and information on its work. The Authority must submit an annual report with audited accounts within six months of the financial year end. The Cabinet Secretary must table this report in Parliament within three months of receipt.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIAs as it establishes reporting obligations that may impact data sharing with Kenyan authorities, particularly for financial institutions.

requirements.text: The Cabinet Secretary may, from time to time, direct the Authority to furnish in such form as he or she may require, returns, accounts and any other information with respect to the work of the Authority and the Authority shall comply with such direction.

The Authority shall, within six months after the close of each financial year, submit to the Cabinet Secretary a report of its operations and activities throughout the year together with audited accounts in such form and detail as the Cabinet Secretary shall, from time to time, determine.
The Cabinet Secretary shall table the report submitted under subsection (3) before above to Parliament within three months of its submission.

sections.source_quote: The Authority shall, within six months after the close of each financial year, submit to the Cabinet Secretary a report of its operations and activities throughout the year together with audited accounts in such form and detail as the Cabinet Secretary shall, from time to time, determine.
